title: Job 18
subtitle: Bildad’s Second Speech: The Fate of the Wicked

heb.paragraph: "The lamp of the wicked is snuffed out" (v.5). The lamp represents life, prosperity, and hope. Bildad says the wicked’s lamp goes dark — their apparent blessing is temporary.

refs.note: "The lamp of the wicked is snuffed out" — the same image Bildad draws on.

notes.note: "When will you end these speeches?" Bildad is exasperated.
"The lamp of a wicked man is snuffed out. His vigorous stride is narrowed; his own schemes bring him down. His feet thrust him into a net; he wanders into its mesh."
Calvin: the lamp imagery is proverbial truth. The wicked’s prosperity does end. But Bildad applies it specifically to Job, which is a misapplication.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET: "the lamp" is a common metaphor for the life or prosperity of a family line (cf. 2 Sam 21:17).

clines.source: David J.A. Clines, Job, WBC (3 vols., 1989–2011) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Clines: Bildad’s speech is notable for what it lacks: argument. There is no theology, no reasoning. It is pure assertion — a verbal avalanche aimed at burying Job.

habel.source: Norman C. Habel, The Book of Job, OTL (1985) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Habel: Bildad’s second testimony is a closing argument. No new evidence, no new theory — just rhetorical intimidation.

hist.context: Bildad opens aggressively: “When will you end these speeches?” (v.2). He then launches into the imagery of the wicked’s destruction: their lamp is snuffed, their steps are narrowed, their own schemes trip them up. Snares, traps, and nets — the wicked are caught by their own devices.

heb.paragraph: "Terrors (balhōṭ) startle him on every side and dog his every step" (v.11). Bildad’s speech is a single sustained portrait of the wicked person’s destruction. The terrors are comprehensive: physical, social, familial. The wicked person loses everything — and Bildad intends Job to see himself in the mirror.

"The king of terrors (melek̅ balhōṭ)" (v.14) — a personification of death itself. Death is the ultimate monarch before whom all others bow. The phrase may allude to Mot, the Canaanite god of death, or Nergal, the Mesopotamian ruler of the underworld.

refs.note: "Like sheep they are destined for the grave, and death will be their shepherd" — death as ruler over the wicked.
"The lamp of the wicked is snuffed out" — the same image Bildad uses (v.5).

notes.note: "When will you end these speeches?" Bildad is exasperated. He accuses Job of treating the friends like animals.
"The lamp of a wicked man is snuffed out; the flame of his fire stops burning." The lamp image: the wicked’s prosperity is a candle that will be extinguished.
Terrors on every side, disease eating his skin, marched before the king of terrors, sulphur scattered on his dwelling. The imagery is apocalyptic in its comprehensiveness.

Roots dry up below, branches wither above. No offspring, no survivor. His name vanishes. Everyone is appalled. Bildad ends: "Surely such is the dwelling of an evil man; such is the place of one who does not know God." Direct accusation.

Calvin: the “king of terrors” is death personified. Bildad uses the image to frighten Job into repentance. But Job has already said he longs for death (ch 3). The threat has no power over someone who welcomes its fulfilment.
Calvin: “one who does not know God” is the harshest charge a believer can make against another. Bildad has crossed from theological disagreement to personal damnation.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET: “the firstborn of death” devours his skin — possibly a reference to a particularly virulent disease, personified as death’s offspring.
The NET: “king of terrors” may allude to the ANE death-deity. The phrase occurs nowhere else in the OT.

clines.source: David J.A. Clines, Job, WBC (3 vols., 1989–2011) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Clines: “the place of one who does not know God” is Bildad’s final verdict. He has moved from “does God pervert justice?” (8:3) to an outright accusation that Job does not know God.

habel.source: Norman C. Habel, The Book of Job, OTL (1985) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Habel: Bildad’s second testimony is a closing argument. No new evidence, no new theory — just rhetorical intimidation. The prosecution has nothing left but volume.

hist.context: Bildad’s second speech is shorter and harsher than his first. No appeal to tradition this time — just a relentless catalogue of the wicked person’s destruction. The structure: terrors pursue (vv.5–12), disease consumes (vv.13–14), tent destroyed (vv.15–16), name erased (vv.17–18), no offspring survive (v.19), all who see it are appalled (vv.20–21).

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVYou who tear yourself to pieces in your anger, is the earth to be abandoned for your sake? Or must the rocks be moved from their place?ESVYou who tear yourself in your anger, shall the earth be forsaken for you, or the rock be removed out of its place?NIVHis feet thrust him into a net; he wanders into its mesh.ESVFor he is cast into a net by his own feet, and he walks on its mesh.

rec.text: ""When will you end these speeches?" Bildad is exasperated."

rows.label: The Lamp Snuffed — 18:1–10
rows.text: Bildad opens aggressively. The wicked’s lamp goes dark; snares and nets trap them.

rows.label: The King of Terrors — 18:11–21
rows.text: Terrors, disease, death personified. “The place of one who does not know God.”

hebtext.note: "The lamp of the wicked is snuffed out" (v.5). The lamp represents life, prosperity, and hope. Bildad says the wicked’s lamp goes dark — their apparent blessing is temporary.

"Terrors (balhōṭ) startle him on every side and dog his every step" (v.11). Bildad’s speech is a single sustained portrait of the wicked person’s destruction. The terrors are comprehensive: physical, social, familial. The wicked person loses everything — and Bildad intends Job to see himself in the mirror.

"The king of terrors (melek̅ balhōṭ)" (v.14) — a personification of death itself. Death is the ultimate monarch before whom all others bow. The phrase may allude to Mot, the Canaanite god of death, or Nergal, the Mesopotamian ruler of the underworld.

"Like sheep they are destined for the grave, and death will be their shepherd" — death as ruler over the wicked.
"The lamp of the wicked is snuffed out" — the same image Bildad uses (v.5).

themes.note: Judgment peaks: Bildad’s speech is unrelenting condemnation with no room for mercy or nuance.
